Beginning on February 3rd, numerous rounds of rainfall began to impact eastern Kentucky. This continued through February 7th, highlighted by heavy rainfall from the evening of February 5th through the first half of February 6th. Rain amounts of 4-6 inches fell across much of southeastern Kentucky over this period. This led to major flooding and numerous mudslides across portions of Whitley, Perry, Letcher, Leslie, Knox, Harlan, Clay, and Bell Counties. The Governor subsequently declared a State of Emergency for these 8 counties.||Major flooding occurred along the Cumberland River at Pineville, Barbourville, and Williamsburg. Additionally, the communities of Loyall and Baxter experienced major flooding. Numerous roads became impassable across large portions of Whitley, Knox, Clay, Leslie, Letcher, Harlan, Perry, and Bell counties. In particular, Harlan County was hit extremely hard with the Kentucky Mesonet site on Black Mountain (4031 foot elevation) receiving over 6.5 inches of rain over this stretch. ||Flood gates were closed in Pineville and Harlan, further impacting local travel. A couple of mobile homes were flooded and swept down the Cumberland River in Harlan County, while swift water rescues took place in Bell and Harlan Counties. Elkhorn Dam in Jenkins had water rushing over it for a period of time on February 6. A man went missing in Whitley County and was later found deceased in his car on Kentucky Highway 779 after his attempt to drive into flood waters. A total of seven homes were reported to have been in or under water across Whitley County as the Cumberland River in Williamsburg crested to its third highest stage on record of 34.84 feet. The city of Pineville saw the river rise to its second highest crest in history at 1019.06 feet, leading to several families being stranded without homes.||While impacts were certainly greatest along the Cumberland River, several portions of the Kentucky River also flooded. Several roads were impacted in Hazard and Whitesburg, while Booneville, Heidelberg, and Ravenna also exceeded flood stage. Along the Levisa Fork of the Big Sandy River, a rarity occurred as Pikeville hit flood stage and forced the city to shut its flood gates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported devastating flooding throughout Bell County, including damage to approximately 90 homes. This includes Pineville, where the Cumberland River crested to its second highest stage in history, forcing officials to close the city's floodgates. The community of Fourmile was hard hit, while the Dorton Branch community saw several people without homes as flood waters of nearly a foot deep impacted them. Several homes and businesses, including Faith Missionary Baptist Church, sustained damage. Middlesboro was also heavily impacted as numerous businesses and homes were inundated. At least three water rescues were made by public safety officials between Pineville and Middlesboro.

A deep 500 mb trough over the southwestern states lifted northeast across the central states. A surface low passed east across the lower Mississippi Valley, producing a swath of heavy rain across the lower Ohio Valley. A plume of very moist air aloft accompanied the upper-level trough as it lifted northeast. Although rainfall amounts were not especially heavy, they fell on very moist ground that was primed for flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the road at Highway 299 and Highway 94 East in Murray. The road was nearly impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a Madisonville shopping mall facing Interstate 69 was flooded. Police were called to conduct a water rescue from a stranded vehicle in deep water. The vehicle was towed from the water. Most of the flooding was in commercial parking lots around the mall. Police diverted traffic from the flooding using patrol cars and high water signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front moved southeast across the lower Ohio Valley, preceded by strengthening moisture transport at 850 mb. The parent 500 mb trough moved into the central and southern Plains. This system brought heavy rainfall to parts of western Kentucky. Some of the heaviest rainfall amounts were in the Paducah area, where around 2.5 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some creeks came out of their banks, including Perkins Creek in Paducah. Water was over some roads in typical low-lying and poor drainage areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet February contributed to another period of river flooding. This was the 8th wettest February on record in Paducah, where the monthly rainfall total was 6.98 inches. Moderate flooding was observed along the Green River. Considerable flooding of fields occurred. Several secondary state roads were flooded or closed.  The Green River crested on the 16th at Paradise at 388.92 feet, which is almost nine feet above flood stage. This was about four and a half feet below the flood crest of February, 2019. The crest at Calhoun occurred on the 18th at 27.25 feet, which is four and a half feet above flood stage. This was a few feet lower than the 2019 flood at Calhoun.

Beginning Monday, February 10, light rain moved back into eastern Kentucky. This caused additional issues, while another round on Wednesday, February 12 into Thursday, February 13 brought continued river flooding along points on the Kentucky and Cumberland Rivers. Gusty winds up to 40 to 45 mph resulted in an isolated downed tree in the Blackey community of Letcher County due to the saturated nature of the ground, as well as a children's wooden playground being flipped and tossed 40-50 feet from its origin near Kernie in Magoffin County. In addition to high water on area roads, mudslides also continued to impact multiple highways. One of these near Williamson in northeast Pike County on Kentucky Highway 292 resulted in a multi day road closure. ||The town of Confluence in Leslie County once again experienced flooding issues as water levels rose at Buckhorn Lake. This impacted spots along Kentucky Highway 257, including homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Transportation Cabinet relayed a report of a rockslide, due to previous heavy rain, on Kentucky Highway 292 between U.S. Highway 119 and ARH Hospital near Williamson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning Monday, February 10, light rain moved back into eastern Kentucky. This caused additional issues, while another round on Wednesday, February 12 into Thursday, February 13 brought continued river flooding along points on the Kentucky and Cumberland Rivers. Gusty winds up to 40 to 45 mph resulted in an isolated downed tree in the Blackey community of Letcher County due to the saturated nature of the ground, as well as a children's wooden playground being flipped and tossed 40-50 feet from its origin near Kernie in Magoffin County. In addition to high water on area roads, mudslides also continued to impact multiple highways. One of these near Williamson in northeast Pike County on Kentucky Highway 292 resulted in a multi day road closure. ||The town of Confluence in Leslie County once again experienced flooding issues as water levels rose at Buckhorn Lake. This impacted spots along Kentucky Highway 257, including homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a train derailment as a result of an active mudslide, caused by previous heavy rainfall, near Draffin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and surface cold front moved through the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours on the 7th, leading to heavy, wet snow that impacted the morning commute. A brief period of heavy snow fell during the morning of the 7th with lighter snow continuing into the early afternoon. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 3 inches in eastern Kentucky. This resulted in delayed openings and closures of schools.

Scattered storms were ongoing across portions of eastern Kentucky during the evening hours on July 26. Without much in the way of steering winds aloft, these storms had very little storm motion, and were capable of producing heavy rainfall over isolated areas throughout their duration. One of these storms occurred on the Harlan/Letcher County line, exacerbated by the steeper mountainous terrain. Flash flooding was reported in and around the Cumberland community of Harlan County, where radar estimates between 2 and 3 inches fell during the lifetime of this nearly-stationary storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell in the Cumberland community of Harlan County. City police reported high water across a handful of streets in the community, including multiple road closures.  College road along the south side of town was among those listed. The police department also reported that one of their police cruisers was stuck in the flood waters and would not start.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearby cool front combined with a hot and humid air mass to spark a broken line of showers and storms across northern Kentucky during the afternoon of the 20th. One of the storms produced severe wind gusts across Morgan County. This area of showers and storms diminished thereafter as a southward-propagating outflow boundary outpaced the line of convection. Additional showers and storms, one of which produced severe winds and nickel-sized hail in McCreary County, developed along and just ahead of the outflow boundary across portions of southeast Kentucky during the evening hours. Convection quickly diminished by sunset with the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McCreary County 911 Call Center reported a tree down along TJ King Rd northeast of Pine Knot.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four workers were injured from a lightning strike at the Cincinnati Northern Kentucky International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in the heat of the afternoon across the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. Isolated strong winds, frequent lightning, and very heavy rain accompanied the storms. A weak low pressure system over the Tennessee Valley contributed to the formation of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon along an east to west corridor across southeast Missouri into western Kentucky. The storms produced very heavy rainfall, along with an isolated damaging wind gust or two. The storms formed along and ahead of a slow-moving frontal boundary that extended generally along the Ohio River into southeast Missouri. The environment near and south of the front was characterized by dew points in the lower to middle 70's and precipitable water values around 1.75 inches. With daytime heating, instability increased substantially to around 3000 J/kg south of the front, with a sharp gradient along the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down around the junction of State Routes 56 and 758.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed this afternoon, with one briefly strengthening to severe limits in Wayne County. As it did so, trees were downed at a residence on the west side of Monticello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed trees downed on Pearl Smith Road on the west side of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorm cells over saturated ground caused localized flash flooding during the afternoon hours. An upper-level disturbance advancing quickly southeast across western Illinois promoted the growth of thunderstorms along an outflow boundary extending from southwest Illinois across western Kentucky. Very unstable air existed on the warm west side of the boundary, with dew points in the upper 70's. Weak low level inflow of this very unstable air ascended across the boundary, supporting active convection east of the boundary. Storm motions were nearly stationary at times, resulting in 2 to 3 inch totals over a 1 to 2 hour period. Since the ground was saturated, some flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A secondary road was water-covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered  to numerous showers and thunderstorms were ongoing during the early afternoon hours on July 23rd. One of these storms, located in Laurel County, near London, had some stronger winds located along the leading edge. This wind gust resulted in a downed tree across a road near London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared a picture taken by a public source of one downed pine tree across Clear Springs Dr near the intersection of Summer Springs Dr, about 4.2 miles south of London. The damage occurred along the leading edge of an incoming storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a hot and sultry afternoon, storms started developing over northeast Tennessee and along the KY/VA border. These storm clusters generated a northeast and northwest propagating outflow boundary which collided across portions of southern KY, generating additional strong thunderstorm activity. The highest measured wind gust was 38 mph at the Williamsburg Airport. Isolated tree damage was reported in Knox County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Knox County State Highway Garage reported a tree downed by thunderstorm winds near KY-1803 northwest of Jarvis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a warm and unstable air mass during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed along State Route 368.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a surface warm front that lifted slowly north across western Kentucky and southern Illinois in the afternoon. Soils were quite saturated from recent heavy rains, causing rapid runoff and isolated flash flooding. Precipitable water values ranged from 2 to 2.25 inches along the front. The heavy rain potential was limited by weak 850 mb winds around 10 knots. Strong instability contributed to a few severe storms that briefly pulsed above severe limits. Weak winds aloft limited the potential for organized strong or severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded on the west side of Hopkinsville. Water was threatening a few homes as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Green River fell below flood stage on the first day of the month following a wet June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Green River inundated some low-lying cropland and fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary across the region, in addition to warm and humid conditions, created an atmosphere conducive to showers and thunderstorms. Without much wind shear, most of these storms were short lived and slow moving. However, a few storms did sustain themselves and become strong enough to produce strong to severe wind gusts, resulting in several downed trees where they occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported that thunderstorms winds blew a tree down near the Flat Lick Community of Knox County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moved through northern Floyd County early during the 7 PM hour. Although it was short lived and not of severe strength, a burst of stronger winds occurred in an isolated area west of Prestonsburg as the storm began to dissipate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A burst of wind occurred in the Woodland Hills subdivision west of Prestonsburg, downing several trees and blocking the roadway. Large limb debris was also observed throughout the subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the Mississippi River counties and the Mayfield area of extreme western Kentucky, heat indices peaked from 105 to 110 degrees in the afternoon. A hot and humid southwest wind prevailed ahead of a cold front extending from the St. Louis area to the Ozark Mountains of southwest Missouri.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous city streets and county roads were flooded, with estimated water depths from six inches to one foot. State Highway 121 was flooded south of New Concord. Creeks were out of their banks. At the Kentucky mesonet site near downtown Murray, 2.06 inches fell in 35 minutes. A water rescue was conducted west of Hazel along the state line, where a vehicle drove into floodwaters and stalled. Near Midway off U.S. Highway 641, two homes were evacuated due to flooding. Water was 4 to 8 inches deep in the homes. Some roads remained barricaded shut due to flooding well into the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With zonal flow over the country and high temperatures in the 90s, multiple weak surface fronts help to produce afternoon and evening storms during diurnal heating. These storms produced strong winds, hail, and heavy rainfall that resulted in wind damage and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was flash flooding in the Ten Broeck subdivision near Highway 22 and Ten Broeck Way.

This produced several thunderstorms that caused widespread damage throughout the Bluegrass region of Kentucky. The Kentucky Mesonet  recorded a 77 mph wind gust within one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Highway 388 in Madison.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge was closed on Salem Church Road due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Kentucky sat under the front of an upper ridge as a surface low advanced toward the state. This low anchored a warm front to the east and cold front to the west and caused thunderstorms to breakout across central Kentucky as the system moved through the area. The storms produced wind and hail damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was ripped out of the ground, and a large oak tree limb was broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain developed over western Kentucky on consecutive afternoons. The storms formed in response to daytime heating of a moist and unstable air mass. A southwest wind flow prevailed ahead of a cold front as it sagged southeast across Missouri and Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closures were reported in the city of Paducah. At least a couple feet of water covered those roads. Nearby rain gages measured about three inches of rain in 45 minutes.

Snowfall accumulations through midnight ranged from one to three inches and continued into Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 2 miles south-southwest of Florence measured 3 inches of snow while another 3 miles south-southeast of town measured 2.5 inches. The observer in Burlington measured 1.9 inches and the CVG airport measured 1.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated small clusters of thunderstorms formed during the heat of the afternoon. A 500 mb trough digging southeast across the Great Lakes region forced a cold front southeastward into southern Illinois by evening. The storms ahead of this front produced locally heavy rain and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four power poles were down on U.S. Highway 60 on the west side of Reed. Highway 60 was closed for several hours. A tree was down a couple miles southwest of Spottsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a time without any major weather systems moving through the region, a moist atmosphere with differential heating and remaining outflows from earlier thunderstorms was enough for thunderstorms and heavy rain to develop across central Kentucky. This resulted in widespread wind damage, flooding, and even fires from lightning. An elderly man drowned and his wife was injured trying to drive through flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was water in one home, and a private bridge to get to other houses was under water on Rush Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving thunderstorms developed near a stationary front that extended from southeast Missouri across western Kentucky. A well-defined upper-level low circulation over eastern Kansas moved east, providing some additional support for the storms. The precipitable water values across the region were on the order of 1.8 to 2.0 inches, which supported heavy rainfall rates that locally approached or exceeded 1.5 inches per hour with the strongest convective cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street and yard flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a surface warm front that lifted slowly north across western Kentucky and southern Illinois in the afternoon. Soils were quite saturated from recent heavy rains, causing rapid runoff and isolated flash flooding. Precipitable water values ranged from 2 to 2.25 inches along the front. The heavy rain potential was limited by weak 850 mb winds around 10 knots. Strong instability contributed to a few severe storms that briefly pulsed above severe limits. Weak winds aloft limited the potential for organized strong or severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house near Hickory was surrounded by water. Highway 339 was closed by high water east of Sedalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon along an east to west corridor across southeast Missouri into western Kentucky. The storms produced very heavy rainfall, along with an isolated damaging wind gust or two. The storms formed along and ahead of a slow-moving frontal boundary that extended generally along the Ohio River into southeast Missouri. The environment near and south of the front was characterized by dew points in the lower to middle 70's and precipitable water values around 1.75 inches. With daytime heating, instability increased substantially to around 3000 J/kg south of the front, with a sharp gradient along the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some roads were flooded, mainly in far western sections of McCracken County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few clusters of severe thunderstorms moved northwest out of Tennessee into the Purchase area of western Kentucky. Sporadic wind damage was reported west of Kentucky Lake. The storms occurred ahead of the remnants of Hurricane Laura, which were centered over Arkansas. The storms were embedded in a deep southeast wind flow ahead of Laura's remnants. Instability was sufficient for strong to severe storms. Mixed-layer capes were over 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near Fairdealing. Numerous small limbs, an inch or less in size, were down along Highway 402 between Aurora and Hardin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was stalled across the Central Appalachians, with a convergence boundary also located across the eastern portion of Kentucky. These boundaries combined with afternoon heating/moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms, which propogated into far eastern Kentucky. Even heading into the late evening hours, instability was still sufficient enough to keep storms strong, producing heavy rain and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report came in on social media showing an automated weather station and rain gauge that had measured 3.95 inches of rain during the heavy rain event. It was also noted that of that amount, 3.5 inches had fallen in the first 1 hour.

As a result, rainfall rates in thunderstorms were as much as two to three inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface frontal boundary was moving through eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on Tuesday, August 18. The influx of moisture and instability led to showers and thunderstorms developing across the northern half of the state, and then sinking southward throughout the mid afternoon and evening. A storm developed across McCreary county during the early evening hours, intensifying enough to result in very heavy rain, frequent lightning, and pea to dime size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of hail, ranging from pea to dime size, was made as the storm went through the Marshes Siding community during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced very heavy rain across the lower Ohio Valley. The storms occurred in a moist and unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving 500 mb disturbance over south central Illinois. The slow-moving storms initiated along a weak|low level convergence axis stretching from east central Missouri into southern Illinois and northwestern Kentucky. Precipitable water values across the region ranged from 1.6 to 2.0 inches, supporting locally heavy rainfall rates near two inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred near the Kentucky Wesleyan College campus in Owensboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was stalled across the Central Appalachians, with a convergence boundary also located across the eastern portion of Kentucky. These boundaries combined with afternoon heating/moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms, which propogated into far eastern Kentucky. Even heading into the late evening hours, instability was still sufficient enough to keep storms strong, producing heavy rain and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car became stranded in the roadway due to high waters near Orchard Branch off of Highway 426 in the community of Dana. The vehicle was rescued before deputies arrived to help.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a cold front that extended from the upper Mississippi Valley to the central Plains, hot and humid southwest winds prevailed. Afternoon heat indices peaked from 105 to 110 degrees in most areas.

A stationary frontal boundary was located across portions of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening of 8/13/2020. This created the epicenter for shower and thunderstorm development, especially along and south of the boundary, where warm moist air was abundant. Heavy rains that occurred in Pike and Floyd Counties were enough to lead to some flooding issues, where Dual Pol radar estimated between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 Dispatch reported heavy rains have led to water across Chloe Road in Pikeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary was located generally west to east across central and eastern Kentucky. Decent warm air and moisture advection was present south of the boundary, and provided the ingredients necessary to produce low centroid high precipitating showers and storms throughout the afternoon hours. Heavy rain set up across portions of Breathitt County, including the city center of Jackson, and remained in place for an extended period amount of time. This resulted in flash flooding and general areal flooding as creeks extended out of their banks and onto nearby roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused water to rise quickly at the Happy Church in the Quicksand community just outside of Jackson. An NWS employee was on site and said it eventually surrounded a portion of a home on the property, and then began seeping through a ground-level door into the walkout basement, flooding the basement. The nearby mesonet reported 2.30 inches of rainfall during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was progressing eastward through eastern Kentucky on the afternoon of Sunday August 16, 2020. The influx of moisture and instability along and near the front resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the region. One such storm moved through Breathitt County, intensifying and resulting in high wind gusts, along with heavy rain and frequent lightning, across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts on the leading edge of the storm led to a tree being downed along Hwy 540/Strong Fork Road just south of Jackson in the Quicksand community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in close proximity to a weak 500 mb low over the lower Ohio Valley. Low level lapse rates were quite steep, near 7 degrees Celsius/km. A short-lived funnel cloud formed in association with one of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was witnessed by several observers near the Owensboro airport. The airport control tower observer reported the funnel cloud lasted three minutes. The rotating funnel was described as thin and wispy, attached to the cloud base. Trees obstructed the control tower's view of the lowest parts of the funnel cloud. No confirmation of any touchdown or damage was obtained. The funnel cloud occurred with a thunderstorm that occurred in a weakly sheared environment with no history of sustained strong updrafts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few clusters of severe thunderstorms moved northwest out of Tennessee into the Purchase area of western Kentucky. Sporadic wind damage was reported west of Kentucky Lake. The storms occurred ahead of the remnants of Hurricane Laura, which were centered over Arkansas. The storms were embedded in a deep southeast wind flow ahead of Laura's remnants. Instability was sufficient for strong to severe storms. Mixed-layer capes were over 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were snapped, resulting in several road closures. Trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture anchored across east Kentucky, combined with a stalled frontal boundary, then a cold front, led to several rounds of heavy rain from the evening of September 12th through the early afternoon hours on September 13th. This rain led to many reports of flooding and flash flooding, with several water rescues occurring during the late evening hours of September 12th. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 5.5 inches in the area where the heaviest rains fell.||In Powell county, at the Middlefork Campground, a truck was caught up in flood waters, but all passengers were able to get to safety. Powell County search and rescue were also involved in getting people to safety in the Indian Creek area of the Red River Gorge.||In Menifee county, 30 to 40 campers had to seek higher ground as the Red River Adventure Campground saw extensive flooding.  Two other campers had to be rescued who were camping near Brushy Creek in the northern part of the county.||In Morgan county, the cow branch community saw extensive flooding, along with significant flooding in and around West Liberty.  Several roads sustained damage in West Liberty.  There was also extensive flooding along Spaws Creek. Bethany Enterprise Baptist Church along war creek also saw some damage to their property.||Finally, in Jackson county, there were several instances of roads being shut down due to flash flooding, especially late Sunday morning into Sunday afternoon with a second surge in rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert was washed out on Opossum Hollow Road due to high waters. The road will remain closed until repairs can be made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms blossomed and moved along a nearly stalled front that extended from east to west across the state of Kentucky. A southwest low-level jet around 40 knots brought increasing moisture and instability. Precipitable water values measured by satellite observations were as high as 2.4 inches in western Kentucky, with more widespread values from 1.8 to 2.1 inches across the area. These precipitable water values were above the 90th percentile, and in some areas above the daily record for September 3. In areas of training convection, rainfall rates reached two inches per hour despite fast movement of individual cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets were flooded in the city of Owensboro and outside the city. Manhole covers were lifted off. Vehicles were stranded. One automated rain gage in the Owensboro city limits and a second gage east of the city measured about four inches during the night. Of that rainfall total, about two inches fell in an hour when the flash flooding started.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on September 2nd, a couple of cold fronts with embedded lows headed south towards central Kentucky. The first of these front passed through Kentucky on the 2nd. This brought isolated severe wind damage and flooding, but as the second low/cold front moved through on the 3rd, heavy rainfall caused urban flooding issues across Jefferson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from Caney Creek was flowing over Franklin Gaynor Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 13th, a large cold front covering the eastern United Stated slide southeast through the Lower Ohio Valley. This caused widespread rainfall. Areas in Casey County received over 4 inches of rainfall, causing many parts of Liberty and surrounding areas to flood. Several roadways were closed, and multiple structures were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upper Brush Creek Road flooded in multiple locations. Flowing debris was observed near the intersection of Highway 1552.

Areas in Casey County received over 4 inches of rainfall, causing many parts of Liberty and surrounding areas to flood. Several roadways were closed, and multiple structures were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in Liberty along the Green River. US 127 and Highway 70 west-southwest of Liberty were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through east Kentucky on Christmas Eve.  Widespread rain accompanied the cold front through early afternoon.  However, as the cold air spread into east Kentucky, it interacted with increasing moisture from a weather disturbance aloft, to provide a changeover to snow that occurred between 2 pm and 8 pm.  A short period of sleet was also observed during the changeover time.  Snow became very heavy at times with snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour observed.  The snow continued into the evening hours, before the widespread snow pulled out of the area.  However, snow showers persisted from Christmas Eve Night through Christmas day, before finally winding down.  As temperatures plummeted into the teens and 20s during the evening on Christmas Eve, icy conditions quickly developed areawide.  These icy conditions continued through Christmas.  ||The most notable impact was related to travel across east Kentucky.  The heavy snow created treacherous road conditions across east Kentucky.  The Hal Rogers Parkway was shut down between London and Manchester for a short time.  There were also numerous reports of cars off the road and significant backups due to poor weather conditions.  In addition, there were many reports of trees knocked down due to the heavy snow along with thousands of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were made from across the county ranging from 6 to 8 inches from late Christmas Eve through Christmas morning. Of the highest reports, 8 inches in Bulan at 10:22pm EST on 12/24, and two 7.5 inch reports came in near Hazard and the community of Jeff at 6:50 am EST on 12/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a time without any major weather systems moving through the region, a moist atmosphere with differential heating and remaining outflows from earlier thunderstorms was enough for thunderstorms and heavy rain to develop across central Kentucky. This resulted in widespread wind damage, flooding, and even fires from lightning. An elderly man drowned and his wife was injured trying to drive through flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down on Rough Ridge Road and Dry Fork Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front as it sliced eastward across Missouri during the day, reaching the lower Ohio Valley in the evening. Gusts over 40 mph were recorded in a few places in the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. The highest measured gust was 45 mph at a mesonet site near Marion in Crittenden County. A gust to 44 mph was clocked at a mesonet site near Cadiz in Trigg County. The peak gust at the Kentucky mesonet site near Calhoun was 40 mph.

This resulted in widespread wind damage, flooding, and even fires from lightning. An elderly man drowned and his wife was injured trying to drive through flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driver on Lawrenceburg Road noticed a white Ford Explorer that had washed off the road in flood waters. He then noticed an elderly man and woman were still in the vehicle with water up to their necks.  Rescuers broke out windows on the vehicle and pulled the couple out just as it filled completely with flood water. Both were unconscious when they were pulled out; the woman was resuscitated at the scene, but her husband never regained consciousness and died later that afternoon after they were both transported to a local hospital.

A strong cold front passed through east Kentucky on Christmas Eve.  Widespread rain accompanied the cold front through early afternoon.  However, as the cold air spread into east Kentucky, it interacted with increasing moisture from a weather disturbance aloft, to provide a changeover to snow that occurred between 2 pm and 8 pm.  A short period of sleet was also observed during the changeover time.  Snow became very heavy at times with snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour observed.  The snow continued into the evening hours, before the widespread snow pulled out of the area.  However, snow showers persisted from Christmas Eve Night through Christmas day, before finally winding down.  As temperatures plummeted into the teens and 20s during the evening on Christmas Eve, icy conditions quickly developed areawide.  These icy conditions continued through Christmas.  ||The most notable impact was related to travel across east Kentucky.  The heavy snow created treacherous road conditions across east Kentucky.  The Hal Rogers Parkway was shut down between London and Manchester for a short time.  There were also numerous reports of cars off the road and significant backups due to poor weather conditions.  In addition, there were many reports of trees knocked down due to the heavy snow along with thousands of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged throughout the county from just a couple inches up to 5 inches. Highest amounts included a report of 5 inches at both the community of Whitepost (1202 am EST on 12/25) and near the community of Regina (1148 pm EST on 12/24).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through eastern Kentucky Sunday morning, November 15th, 2020. A short period of sleet was also observed during the changeover time.  Snow became very heavy at times with snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour observed.  The snow continued into the evening hours, before the widespread snow pulled out of the area.  However, snow showers persisted from Christmas Eve Night through Christmas day, before finally winding down.  As temperatures plummeted into the teens and 20s during the evening on Christmas Eve, icy conditions quickly developed areawide.  These icy conditions continued through Christmas.  ||The most notable impact was related to travel across east Kentucky.  The heavy snow created treacherous road conditions across east Kentucky.  The Hal Rogers Parkway was shut down between London and Manchester for a short time.  There were also numerous reports of cars off the road and significant backups due to poor weather conditions.  In addition, there were many reports of trees knocked down due to the heavy snow along with thousands of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was made of 9.2 inches by a member of the public 2 miles ENE of the community of Florress around 9:00 pm EST on 12/24, followed by a measured report of 7.0 inches passed along by broadcast media in West Liberty at 10:23 pm EST.

A cold front arrived into eastern Kentucky on the warm afternoon of June 10th, bringing with it strong thunderstorms. These storms inflicted strong winds and a few instances of small hail across the region. Downed trees occurred with the strong thunderstorm winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the town of Ulysses, several trees were downed onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a stalled frontal boundary which was draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of downed trees were received in the Foster Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed this afternoon and evening across much of eastern Kentucky. One of these initially became strong enough to produce a localized downburst near Bowen, while a storm also briefly became strong enough to knock some small trees and limbs down in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen and local media outlet relayed a report of a few trees downed at Callies Lake and Campground near Bowen. A structure was damaged by one of these trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very weak flow aloft and an anomalously unstable and moist environment along a residual outflow boundary promoted the development of thunderstorms across the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys through the late afternoon into mid evening. A weak shortwave slowly advanced eastward along the bootheel of Missouri, while a weak impulse moved southeast from the northern border of Missouri. An outflow boundary was the primary focus for renewed development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, and an isolated damaging microburst occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county road was closed by flash flooding. Several other roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low in Wisconsin deepened and ejected northeast into Canada. A convective line with damaging thunderstorms occurred as a cold front crossed the region. Afterwards in the cold advection behind the front, damaging winds also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous people were without power in Hebron and Union due to power lines and poles that were downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front by mid-late afternoon across central Kentucky, moving into eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. Initially, many of these storms contained strong enough outflow winds to produce damage to trees as well as a couple of structures. As these storms moved farther east of U.S. Highway 23 into the mid evening hours closer to sunset, they weakened below severe limits. Additionally, stronger upper level forcing across northern to central Kentucky versus southern Kentucky helped to spare southeast Kentucky from any damaging winds.||As the cold front moved through during the late evening hours to just after midnight, strong winds accompanied the frontal passage across isolated portions of Floyd and Pike Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple periods of heavy rainfall occurred across eastern Kentucky as an upper level disturbance nudged westward toward the western Appalachians. This resulted in flash flooding across portions of Pulaski and McCreary Counties, while a locally strong to severe thunderstorm occurred near Williamsburg. Additionally, a commercial vehicle was struck by lightning in Whitley County along Interstate 75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed nearly four feet of water flowing over West Coleman Road southwest of Dabney and southeast of Science Hill, with cars attempting to drive through it.

A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, and an isolated damaging microburst occurred.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front by mid-late afternoon across central Kentucky, moving into eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. Initially, many of these storms contained strong enough outflow winds to produce damage to trees as well as a couple of structures. As these storms moved farther east of U.S. Highway 23 into the mid evening hours closer to sunset, they weakened below severe limits. Additionally, stronger upper level forcing across northern to central Kentucky versus southern Kentucky helped to spare southeast Kentucky from any damaging winds.||As the cold front moved through during the late evening hours to just after midnight, strong winds accompanied the frontal passage across isolated portions of Floyd and Pike Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Transportation Cabinet reported a tree downed along Kentucky Highway 89 near Foxtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed this afternoon and evening in the vicinity of a slow-moving upper level low pressure system. Several storms briefly became strong, with one producing large hail across portions of Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citizens observed hail up to the size of quarters from McKee to Tyner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a stalled frontal boundary which was draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of the AA Highway near Foster was washed out. Western Route 8 also had high water and Ruf Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a stalled frontal boundary which was draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of water was flowing down the street and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very weak flow aloft and an anomalously unstable and moist environment along a residual outflow boundary promoted the development of thunderstorms across the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys through the late afternoon into mid evening. A weak shortwave slowly advanced eastward along the bootheel of Missouri, while a weak impulse moved southeast from the northern border of Missouri. An outflow boundary was the primary focus for renewed development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, and an isolated damaging microburst occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six utility poles were broken on the west side of Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A training thunderstorm complex during the night of June 27th into the 28th produced significant flash flooding, as well as minor river flooding on the Green River. The maximum axis of rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches occurred over portions of northwest Kentucky, just south of the cities of Owensboro and Henderson. A weak mid-level disturbance advanced east early in the morning across eastern IL. This energy helped foster thunderstorm activity across portions of southern IL, southern IN, and Kentucky. The air mass around the southern flank of the disturbance was still at least modestly unstable with mixed layer capes around 1000 j/kg early in the morning. A west-southwest low-level wind flow between 30 and 40 knots focused strong moisture transport into the storms. The storms trained over the same area for three to five hours. Precipitable water values across the region were on the order of 1.7 to 1.9 inches, fueling rainfall rates exceeding 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 65 mph was measured at the Kentucky mesonet site in Todd County. A nearby carport structure was blown over.

A state of emergency was declared by county government officials. The mesonet site five miles northwest of Calhoun measured 6.74 inches in 48 hours, but much of that fell on the morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent thunderstorms produced flash flooding in the Owensboro area. A mid-level circulation over southeast Missouri helped to focus shower and thunderstorm activity into southern Illinois and northwest Kentucky.  This circulation was associated with a trough from 850-700 mb.  Out ahead of the trough, the air mass was quite unstable with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 j/kg. Precipitable water values in the axis of instability increased to near 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of numerous roads occurred in the city of Owensboro. Several roads were impassable for an extended period of time in the city and in rural locations west and southwest of the city. Vehicles were stranded in some locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous water rescues were conducted due to vehicles in floodwaters across the county, including the city of Owensboro. A trained spotter at Utica measured 5.34 inches by mid-morning, with rain still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front swept across the region during the afternoon and evening of Christmas Eve. Rain changed to a period of heavy snow during the evening with a period of snowfall rates of near two inches an hour as it passed over Kentucky. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the eastern portions of the state, leading to treacherous road conditions and a few areas of significant car backups on the evening of December 24th. Snow showers remained in the air all of Christmas Day bringing some additional accumulations.  This system produced our first white Christmas in several years for many locations.

A trough of low pressure aloft combined with mid level energy to bring a shot of accumulating snowfall to parts of the Ohio Valley, especially along and east of the Scioto Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Maysville measured 2.8 inches of snowfall on the evening of the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 2 miles south-southwest of Florence measured 3 inches of snow while another 3 miles south-southeast of town measured 2.5 inches. The observer in Burlington measured 1.9 inches and the CVG airport measured 1.4 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer near Wilder measured 1.3 inches, as did the observer located 5 miles northwest of California. A social media report from Alexandria showed that there was an inch of snow.
